content: Just as human language learners build vocabulary mastery by repeatedly reflecting on word usage and meaning to adjust their understanding, large language models can combine the dynamic memory of Reflexion agents with the iterative feedback loop of Self-Refine to correct persistent hallucinations that simple single-pass refinement cannot fix. The long-term memory of prior reflection errors stored by Reflexion acts like a learner's personal vocabulary notebook of past mistakes, allowing Self-Refine's iterative feedback to target repeated errors rather than starting from scratch each iteration.
